content.text: Isolating Python dependencies per project is one of the most fundamental habits in software development — and it becomes critical when building agentic AI systems that pull in a constellation of LLM SDKs, vector database clients, web frameworks, and evaluation tools. A virtual environment gives each project its own private Python interpreter and package set, completely separated from the system installation and from every other project on the same machine.
A Python virtual environment is a self-contained directory tree — commonly named
— that holds its own copy of (or symlink to) a Python interpreter, a standalone
folder. When the environment is activated, the shell prepends its
command operates exclusively inside that environment without touching the global installation.
Internally the environment is created by the
module that ships with Python 3.3 and later (
). The module copies or symlinks the interpreter binary, writes a
file recording the Python version and home directory, and creates the standard directory layout (
itself and its small set of bootstrapping dependencies.
For agentic AI codebases the package surface is broad: the OpenAI or Anthropic SDK, an HTTP client like

, and often tool-use adapters with their own transitive dependencies. Without isolation, installing or upgrading any of these for one project risks breaking another project that pins a different version of the same library.
Python's package manager installs packages into a single flat location by default — the system's

. The moment two projects require different versions of the same library, one of them will break. This collision is endemic in agentic stacks because LLM clients, RAG pipelines, and evaluation harnesses evolve quickly and often pin narrow version ranges for stability.
existed and uses the old completion interface. Without isolation both projects cannot coexist on the same developer workstation or CI runner.

Reproducibility is equally important. CI systems and Docker containers start from a clean state on every run. If local development does not mirror that clean state — because a developer accumulated packages globally over months — test results diverge between local and CI. The 'works on my machine' problem is especially costly for agent evaluations, where subtle numeric differences in library behaviour can flip pass/fail on accuracy metrics.
Security is a third dimension: running
into the system Python on Linux or macOS can corrupt OS tooling that depends on specific versions of Python packages. Virtual environments eliminate the need for elevated privileges entirely —
inside an activated environment never touches system files.
; upgrades in one project cannot shadow packages in another.
produces an exact bill-of-materials that any developer or CI job can reinstall from scratch.
— all package writes go inside the project directory, owned by the current user;
a project can pin 3.10, 3.11, or 3.12 independently; changing one does not affect others.
with no extra packages is only a few MB; startup cost to create one is seconds.
makes it visually obvious which environment is active, preventing silent package mismatch errors.
— VS Code, PyCharm, and similar editors detect
and automatically configure the Python interpreter and linting.
errors or runs the wrong package version silently.
Not portable across OS or architecture
built on macOS cannot be copied to Linux; C extensions are compiled to the host architecture and will not load elsewhere. Always rebuild in CI.
— each project copies or symlinks the same standard library; popular packages like
requires a Python interpreter to already exist; managing multiple Python versions still requires
— if the editor's interpreter path is not updated after creating a new environment, IntelliSense and type-checking silently use the wrong package set.
— environment variables, OS-level secrets, and network access are still shared with the host; virtual environments isolate Python packages only, not the entire runtime context.
module performs several operations in order. First it creates the directory skeleton:
with the interpreter's home path and version number. Third, it either copies or symlinks the Python binary into
— is a shell script that modifies the current shell session. It saves the original
in a temporary variable, then prepends
environment variable to the absolute path of the environment directory and updates the shell prompt. No Python code runs during activation; it is purely a shell-level PATH change.
(a symlink back to the system interpreter) and
resolves to the environment-local pip script. When pip installs a package, it places it in
entry pip consults in this environment.
is constructed at interpreter startup. Inside an activated environment, the interpreter reads
instead of the system's. The system
is excluded by default (controlled by the
). This is the mechanism that makes isolation airtight.

# What 'source .venv/bin/activate' does conceptually:
export VIRTUAL_ENV="/path/to/project/.venv"
_OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH="$PATH"
export PATH="$VIRTUAL_ENV/bin:$PATH"
# (also updates PS1 prompt)

# 'deactivate' reverses this:
export PATH="$_OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH"
unset VIRTUAL_ENV
sys.path inside vs. outside a venv

# Outside any venv:
import sys
print(sys.prefix)        # /usr
print(sys.path[:3])
# ['/usr/lib/python312.zip', '/usr/lib/python3.12', '/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages']

# Inside .venv (after activation):
print(sys.prefix)        # /home/user/agent-platform/.venv
print(sys.path[:3])
# ['', '/home/user/agent-platform/.venv/lib/python3.12',
#  '/home/user/agent-platform/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages']

# Check whether the current process is inside a venv:
in_venv = sys.prefix != sys.base_prefix
print(f"Running in venv: {in_venv}")

The diagram below shows how a virtual environment sits between the system Python interpreter and project-level packages, how PATH manipulation routes all commands through the environment's binaries, and how multiple projects maintain independent

attrs.alt: Virtual environment architecture: .venv directory structure, PATH manipulation on activate, pip install isolation, and multi-project separation

content.text: symlink points back to the system interpreter (no full copy), but its
ensures packages are resolved from the environment's own
to PATH so every subsequent command routes through the environment. Installing packages via the activated pip writes to
Virtual environments should be used for every Python project without exception. The only variance is whether they are managed manually (
) or through a higher-level tool like
in the repo root as the first step after cloning.
— create a fresh environment in every job step to ensure no state leaks between pipeline runs. GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins all support this pattern natively.
Multiple agent services on one machine
— each service directory gets its own environment; developers switch between them by changing directory and activating.
— evaluation scripts often need different SDK versions than production code; separate environments enforce this safely.
— register the environment as a kernel with
so notebooks import from the correct packages.
When standard venv is not the primary mechanism:

If container-based deployment is the delivery model, a Docker image's isolated filesystem serves the same purpose. Inside the container you may still create a venv to improve build caching and layer separation, but the host-level venv discipline matters less. Similarly, for very simple single-file scripts where dependency collision is not a concern, some teams skip the venv overhead — though this is rare in production agentic work.

# Standard approach (Python 3.3+, no additional tools)
cd agent-platform
python3.12 -m venv .venv

# Verify the interpreter symlink
ls -la .venv/bin/python3
# .venv/bin/python3 -> /usr/bin/python3.12

# Linux / macOS (bash or zsh)
source .venv/bin/activate

# macOS fish shell
source .venv/bin/activate.fish

# Windows PowerShell
.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# Windows cmd.exe
.venv\Scripts\activate.bat

# Verify activation
which python      # -> .../agent-platform/.venv/bin/python
echo $VIRTUAL_ENV # -> /absolute/path/to/agent-platform/.venv
Step 3 — Upgrade pip and install dependencies

pip install --upgrade pip
pip install openai httpx fastapi uvicorn pydantic pytest pytest-asyncio deepeval

# Standard pip approach
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# Using uv (recommended for speed):
uv pip compile pyproject.toml -o requirements.lock
In VS Code, open the command palette and run
Example 1 — Quick agent environment from scratch
This script creates a virtual environment, installs the Anthropic SDK, and runs a minimal agent call — all in one go. It can be copy-pasted and run on any machine with Python 3.10+.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -e

python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install --quiet anthropic

python3 - <<'EOF'
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()   # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2 + 2? Reply briefly."}]
)
print(message.content[0].text)
EOF
Example 2 — Multi-service agent project with separate environments

Production agentic systems typically span multiple services. The script below bootstraps three separate environments for an API server, a background worker, and an evaluation harness — each with independent package sets.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# bootstrap.sh — create all three service environments
set -e

services=(
    "agent-api:fastapi uvicorn openai httpx pydantic"
    "agent-worker:celery redis openai httpx"
    "eval-harness:pytest pytest-asyncio deepeval openai"
)

for entry in "${services[@]}"; do
    dir="${entry%%:*}"
    pkgs="${entry##*:}"

    echo "==> Setting up $dir"
    mkdir -p "$dir"
    python3.12 -m venv "$dir/.venv"
    source "$dir/.venv/bin/activate"
    pip install --quiet --upgrade pip
    pip install --quiet $pkgs
    pip freeze > "$dir/requirements.txt"
    deactivate
    echo "    done: $(wc -l < "$dir/requirements.txt") packages pinned"
done

echo ""
echo "Activate individual services with:"
echo "  source agent-api/.venv/bin/activate"
echo "  source agent-worker/.venv/bin/activate"
echo "  source eval-harness/.venv/bin/activate"
Example 3 — Programmatic environment creation in Python
Tools and orchestration scripts sometimes need to create environments programmatically. The
module provides a public Python API:

import venv, subprocess
from pathlib import Path

def create_agent_env(project_dir: Path, packages: list[str]) -> Path:
    """Create a virtual environment and install packages programmatically."""
    venv_path = project_dir / ".venv"

    # Create environment with pip bootstrapped
    builder = venv.EnvBuilder(
        system_site_packages=False,
        clear=False,        # don't wipe if already exists
        symlinks=True,      # symlink interpreter (saves disk)
        upgrade=False,
        with_pip=True,
    )
    builder.create(str(venv_path))

    # Resolve the pip executable inside the new env (no shell activation needed)
    pip_exec = venv_path / "bin" / "pip"

    subprocess.run(
        [str(pip_exec), "install", "--upgrade", "pip", *packages],
        check=True, capture_output=True,
    )
    return venv_path

if __name__ == "__main__":
    env = create_agent_env(
        Path("my-agent"),
        ["openai", "httpx", "pydantic"],
    )
    print(f"Environment ready: {env}")
    print(f"Activate: source {env}/bin/activate")
A production agentic platform consists of three repos:
(FastAPI, handles incoming requests and routes to the correct agent),
against both staging and production agent versions).
Each repo lives in a separate directory with its own
because it needs the newest asyncio performance. The research agent pins
because one of its Playwright bindings did not yet support 3.12 at initial deployment. The eval platform pins
The CI configuration (GitHub Actions) for each repo follows the same pattern:

# .github/workflows/test.yml (research-agent)
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version-file: .python-version   # reads '3.11.9'
          cache: pip
          cache-dependency-path: requirements.txt

      - name: Create virtual environment and install
        run: |
          python -m venv .venv
          source .venv/bin/activate
          pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run tests
        run: |
          source .venv/bin/activate
          pytest evals/ -v --tb=short
Because the CI job installs from a pinned
on every run, there is no state drift between builds. Developers reproduce failures locally by activating their matching
command — the environment is identical to CI because both start from the same lockfile.
The Docker images also follow the venv pattern inside the container, which improves layer caching:

FROM python:3.12-slim AS base

WORKDIR /app

# Dependency layer (cached unless requirements.txt changes)
RUN python -m venv /app/.venv
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN /app/.venv/bin/pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Application code (fast layer — invalidated by code changes only)
COPY src/ ./src/

ENV PATH="/app/.venv/bin:$PATH"
CMD ["uvicorn", "src.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"]

# WRONG: running pytest without activation
cd agent-platform
pytest evals/     # uses /usr/bin/pytest -> imports system packages -> wrong versions

# RIGHT: activate first, then run
source .venv/bin/activate
pytest evals/

# ALTERNATIVE: call the venv interpreter directly without activating
.venv/bin/pytest evals/
.venv/bin/python -m pytest evals/

directory bloats the repository with hundreds of megabytes of compiled binaries. Symlinks inside it are absolute paths that break on any machine other than the one that created the environment. Always add
Mistake 3 — pip install without an active environment

# WRONG — installs into system python
deactivate
pip install openai     # writes to /usr/local/lib/python3.x/

# RIGHT — activate first
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install openai     # writes to .venv/lib/python3.x/site-packages/
Mistake 4 — Stale environment after major dependency changes
After a branch merge that adds many new dependencies or changes major versions, the existing
can hold inconsistent packages. The safest fix is to delete and recreate:

deactivate
rm -rf .venv
python3.12 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
Mistake 5 — Mixing conda and venv on the same project

Activating a conda environment inside a shell that already has a venv active (or vice versa) produces confusing PATH layering. Pick one tool per project and document it in the repo. If conda is needed for scientific dependencies such as NumPy or PyTorch built against specific CUDA versions, use it exclusively rather than mixing.
— place it at the project root so editors and CI scripts find it at a predictable path.
Use direnv or a shell hook to auto-activate
— older pip versions have known resolver bugs; run
Use uv for speed in CI
are 10–100x faster than pip on cold cache; identical virtual environment semantics with much faster pipelines.
for local tooling like pytest and mypy.
Match CI Python version to local
— discrepancies between 3.11 and 3.12 can surface in type-checking, asyncio scheduler changes, and library compatibility.
Do not generate requirements.txt from a dirty environment
— always produce it from a fresh venv with only the intended packages installed.

Virtual environment creation is fast — typically 0.5–2 seconds for an empty environment. The dominant performance variable is package installation time. In CI pipelines with many packages (torch, onnxruntime, chromadb, playwright) this can reach several minutes per run without caching.
— pip maintains a local wheel cache at
by default. Wheels cached from previous runs install in milliseconds instead of downloading from PyPI.
to persist the wheel cache across runs.
— bake a Docker image with all slow-to-build dependencies already installed; downstream CI jobs extend this image.
— creates environments and resolves dependencies far faster than CPython's pip, with no change to the resulting environment structure.
On developer machines with many Python projects,
directories from abandoned projects accumulate. Periodically audit and delete stale
directories. For shared CI runners where disk is a constraint, use
to avoid writing to the wheel cache, accepting slower re-installs.

# Find all .venv directories and their sizes
find ~ -maxdepth 5 -name '.venv' -type d -exec du -sh {} \; 2>/dev/null | sort -h

# Example output:
# 18M   /home/user/eval-harness/.venv
# 45M   /home/user/old-project/.venv
# 210M  /home/user/agent-platform/.venv   (torch + chromadb installed)
module requires no additional installation and is the baseline. It is the right default when the project needs zero extra dependencies for environment management itself.
Developed by Astral (the makers of Ruff),

is a drop-in replacement for pip and venv written in Rust. It resolves, downloads, and installs packages 10–100x faster than pip and creates environments with sub-second latency. Environments created by

# Install uv (standalone installer)
curl -Lsf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Create env and install packages
uv venv .venv
uv pip install openai httpx fastapi

# Compile a lockfile from pyproject.toml
uv pip compile pyproject.toml -o requirements.lock

# Install from lockfile (reproducible)
uv pip sync requirements.lock
manages multiple Python version installations on a single machine. Combined with
, it covers the full workflow: install specific Python versions, set per-directory versions with
, then create environments using the correct interpreter.

# Install pyenv
curl https://pyenv.run | bash

# Install a specific Python version
pyenv install 3.12.3

# Pin this version in the project directory
cd agent-platform
pyenv local 3.12.3      # writes .python-version file

# Create environment using the pinned version
python -m venv .venv    # uses 3.12.3 from pyenv
is a shell extension that reads
files and modifies the shell environment when entering a directory. It eliminates the manual activation step:

# .envrc in the project root
source .venv/bin/activate

# Once-only: allow direnv to load this file
direnv allow

# From now on: cd into the directory and the venv activates automatically
cd agent-platform
# (.venv) prefix appears immediately in the prompt

# Register the venv as a Jupyter kernel so notebooks use it
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install ipykernel
python -m ipykernel install --user \
    --name agent-platform \
    --display-name "Agent Platform (3.12)"

# Launch Jupyter and select the kernel from the notebook UI
jupyter lab
For agentic AI projects the recommended combination is:
for fast environment creation and package installation +
for automatic activation. This setup covers all needs with no compromise.

Virtual environments do not inherently secure the packages they contain — they only isolate them. A malicious or compromised package installed into a venv has full access to the Python runtime and can read environment variables (where API keys typically live), make network requests, and write to the filesystem.
or a lockfile tool so that unexpected upgrades cannot introduce malicious updates.
. This ensures downloaded wheels exactly match the audited artifacts.
package) regularly to surface CVEs in installed packages.
— in enterprise environments, proxy all package downloads through a private mirror (Artifactory, Nexus, CodeArtifact) to scan and approve packages before they reach developer machines.
in production lockfiles; prefer published PyPI releases with verifiable hashes.
— development tools (pytest, mypy, ruff) should never be installed in production images; use a dedicated
— never reuse a saved venv artifact across CI runs; always install from the lockfile on a fresh environment to catch accidental state drift.
— for production services that must never run outside the expected environment, check
at startup to fail fast if the environment was not activated.

Think of the system Python as a shared workshop where any project can borrow tools. A virtual environment is a dedicated toolbox that lives in your project's garage. When you open your garage (activate the venv) you use only the tools in that box — wrenches calibrated exactly the way your project needs them. When a colleague opens their garage, they have their own set of wrenches at different sizes. Leaving a wrench from your garage in the shared workshop (installing globally) risks confusing anyone who picks it up expecting the workshop standard.
and the PATH change work like a mail forwarding address. Every letter (command) first goes to the forwarding address (

) before the postal system (the OS) checks the permanent address (system PATH). When you cancel the forwarding (deactivate), letters go back to the permanent address. No letter was ever lost; only the routing changed.
in every agent project root with
— no exceptions and no global package installs.
or a shell hook to automate it and eliminate the forgetting problem.
and always rebuild from a pinned
In CI, create a fresh environment on every run and install from the exact lockfile — this is the only way to guarantee that local and CI runs are identical.
instead of vanilla pip for 10–100x faster environment creation and package installation with identical semantics.
to pin the Python version per project; record it in
so everyone on the team and CI uses the same interpreter.
Secure the packages inside the environment with hash-verified lockfiles (
Inside Docker, create a venv even within the container — it separates the slow dependency layer from the fast application code layer, improving build cache hit rates.
A virtual environment isolates Python packages, not secrets, network access, or OS resources; apply separate controls for those dimensions.
